cop-001.attackPerspective: Without DLP enforcement, Copilot becomes an uncontrolled data exfiltration tool. A user with broad file access can ask Copilot to "summarize all documents mentioning salary ranges" or "list all credit card numbers in our finance folder" and Copilot will comply — returning sensitive data in a conversational format that bypasses traditional DLP controls on file downloads and email. Attackers with compromised accounts specifically target Copilot for rapid data reconnaissance.
cop-001.implementationNotes:
# Step 1: Connect to Security & Compliance
Connect-IPPSSession

# Step 2: Create DLP policy scoping all M365 workloads
New-DlpCompliancePolicy -Name "Copilot DLP - Block Sensitive Data" `
  -Comment "Prevents Copilot from surfacing regulated data" `
  -ExchangeLocation All -SharePointLocation All `
  -OneDriveLocation All -TeamsLocation All `
  -Mode TestWithNotifications  # Start in test mode

# Step 3: Add rules per sensitive info type
New-DlpComplianceRule -Name "Block Credit Cards in Copilot" `
  -Policy "Copilot DLP - Block Sensitive Data" `
  -ContentContainsSensitiveInformation @{Name="Credit Card Number"; minCount="1"} `
  -BlockAccess $true -NotifyUser "SiteAdmin"

# Step 4: After 2 weeks, switch to enforce
Set-DlpCompliancePolicy -Identity "Copilot DLP - Block Sensitive Data" -Mode Enable

# Step 5: Verify and monitor in DLP Activity Explorer
Get-DlpCompliancePolicy -Identity "Copilot DLP - Block Sensitive Data" | FL Name, Mode, Workload
cop-001.validationNotes:
# Verify DLP policy is active and scoped to Copilot workloads
Get-DlpCompliancePolicy | Where-Object { $_.Name -like "*Copilot*" } |
  Select Name, Mode, Workload, Enabled
# Expected: Mode = Enable, Workload includes SharePointOnline, Exchange, Teams

# Test: Ask Copilot to summarize a document containing test credit card numbers
# Copilot should return a policy tip or redacted response

# Check DLP alerts in Microsoft Purview
# Navigate: Purview portal → Data Loss Prevention → Alerts
cop-001.tuningNotes: Start with high-confidence sensitive info types (credit cards, SSNs) before expanding to custom types. Some organizations need to allow Copilot to reference financial data for authorized users — use scoped DLP policies per user group rather than tenant-wide blocks. Monitor false positive rates in DLP Activity Explorer.
cop-001.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Monitor DLP policy matches on Copilot interactions
DLPEvents
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where Application contains "Copilot" or Application contains "M365Chat"
| summarize MatchCount=count() by UserPrincipalName, PolicyName, SensitiveInfoType
| sort by MatchCount desc

cop-002.attackPerspective: Sensitivity labels are the last line of defense against Copilot data leakage. If Copilot generates a Word document summarizing a "Highly Confidential" source, but the new document has no label, that summary can be freely shared, emailed, or downloaded — bypassing all encryption and access controls on the original. Attackers who compromise a licensed account use Copilot to create unprotected copies of classified content, effectively laundering data through AI.
cop-002.implementationNotes:
# Step 1: Enable AIP integration
Set-SPOTenant -EnableAIPIntegration $true

# Step 2: Verify labels are published
Get-LabelPolicy | Select Name, Labels, ExchangeLocation

# Step 3: Audit EXTRACT right on encrypted labels
Get-Label | Where-Object { $_.EncryptionEnabled -eq $true } | Select Name, EncryptionEnabled

# Step 4: Enable mandatory labeling
Set-LabelPolicy -Identity "<PolicyName>" -AdvancedSettings @{ MandatoryLabelingEnabled="true" }

# Step 5: Deploy auto-labeling in simulation mode for 30 days, then enforce
New-AutoSensitivityLabelPolicy -Name "Auto-Label Copilot Content" `
  -SharePointLocation All -OneDriveLocation All -Mode TestWithNotifications
cop-002.validationNotes:
# Verify AIP integration is enabled
Get-SPOTenant | Select EnableAIPIntegration
# Expected: True

# Check which labels have EXTRACT right restrictions
Get-Label | Where-Object { $_.EncryptionEnabled -eq $true } |
  Select Name, EncryptionEnabled, EncryptionContentExpiredOnDateInDaysOrNever

# Test: Open a "Highly Confidential" encrypted doc, ask Copilot to summarize
# The summary should inherit the HC label and encryption
cop-002.tuningNotes: Auto-labeling policies can generate high false-positive rates on generic content. Scope auto-labeling to specific SharePoint sites or document libraries first. Use simulation mode for 30 days before enforcement. Users who frequently use Copilot for drafting may find mandatory labeling disruptive — provide training and pre-configured label defaults.
cop-002.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Detect Copilot creating files with lower sensitivity than source
CopilotInteractionEvents
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| extend SourceLabel = tostring(parse_json(AccessedResources)[0].SensitivityLabel)
| extend OutputLabel = tostring(parse_json(AuditData).OutputSensitivityLabel)
| where SourceLabel != "" and (OutputLabel == "" or OutputLabel == "General")
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, SourceLabel, OutputLabel

cop-003.attackPerspective: Copilot inherits access through Microsoft Graph — if a compromised account has a valid session from an unmanaged device or untrusted network, Copilot becomes a rapid data extraction tool. An attacker can ask "show me all documents about Project X" from a hotel kiosk. Without CA policies requiring MFA and device compliance for Copilot access, there's no friction between a stolen session token and full organizational data access via AI.
cop-003.validationNotes:
# Verify CA policy is active
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Policy.Read.All"
Get-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy |
  Where-Object { $_.DisplayName -like "*Copilot*" } |
  Select DisplayName, State, GrantControls

# Test from non-compliant device — Copilot should be blocked
# Test from compliant device with MFA — should work

# Check sign-in logs for Copilot access
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn -Filter "appDisplayName eq 'Microsoft 365 Copilot'" -Top 20 |
  Select UserPrincipalName, AppDisplayName, ConditionalAccessStatus, DeviceDetail
cop-003.tuningNotes: Some organizations allow Copilot access from mobile devices via Intune MAM without full device enrollment. In that case, use App Protection Policies as a grant control instead of requiring device compliance. Exclude break-glass accounts but ensure they have separate monitoring.
cop-003.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Detect Copilot access from non-compliant devices
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where AppDisplayName has "Copilot" or ResourceDisplayName has "Copilot"
| where DeviceDetail.isCompliant != true
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, Location, DeviceDetail, ConditionalAccessStatus

cop-004.attackPerspective: Deploying Copilot organization-wide before remediating oversharing is the
cop-004.implementationNotes:
# Step 1: Create Entra ID security group
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Group.ReadWrite.All", "User.ReadWrite.All"
New-MgGroup -DisplayName "Copilot Licensed Users" -MailEnabled:$false `
  -SecurityEnabled:$true -MailNickname "CopilotLicensedUsers"

# Step 2: Use group-based licensing in Entra admin center
# Step 3: Run SharePoint oversharing reports BEFORE each user wave
# Step 4: Add users in waves: IT > Finance > General
# Step 5: Monitor Copilot audit logs after each wave
# Step 6: Set up quarterly access review to recertify licenses
cop-004.validationNotes:
# List all users with Copilot license
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "User.Read.All"
Get-MgUser -Filter "assignedLicenses/any(l:l/skuId eq '<Copilot-SKU-ID>')" -CountVariable count |
  Select DisplayName, UserPrincipalName, Department
Write-Host "Total Copilot users: $count"

# Verify no unintended users have Copilot
# Compare against the approved security group membership
$approved = Get-MgGroupMember -GroupId "<group-id>" | Select -ExpandProperty Id
$licensed = Get-MgUser -Filter "assignedLicenses/any(l:l/skuId eq '<Copilot-SKU-ID>')" | Select -ExpandProperty Id
$unauthorized = $licensed | Where-Object { $_ -notin $approved }
if ($unauthorized) { Write-Warning "Unauthorized Copilot users found: $($unauthorized.Count)" }
cop-004.tuningNotes: Some organizations use self-service license requests via MyAccess portal. If enabled, add an approval workflow requiring manager + IT security sign-off before Copilot access is granted. Set up a recurring access review (quarterly) to remove Copilot licenses from users who have changed roles or left sensitive projects.
cop-004.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Monitor Copilot license assignment changes
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where OperationName == "Change user license"
| extend License = tostring(TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties)
| where License has "Copilot"
| project TimeGenerated, InitiatedBy, TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName, License

cop-005.attackPerspective: Without audit logging, Copilot is a blind spot in your security posture. An insider can use Copilot to query sensitive documents, extract key findings, and exfiltrate data — all without triggering traditional file download alerts. Audit logs capture every Copilot prompt, the resources it accessed, and whether jailbreak attempts were detected. In a real compromise, these logs are the only way to determine what data the attacker accessed via AI. The JailbreakDetected flag specifically catches prompt injection attacks.
cop-005.implementationNotes:
# Step 1: Enable unified audit logging
Connect-IPPSSession
Set-AdminAuditLogConfig -UnifiedAuditLogIngestionEnabled $true

# Step 2: Create retention policy (1 year, E5 required)
New-UnifiedAuditLogRetentionPolicy -Name "Copilot Interactions - 1 Year" `
  -Operations @("CopilotInteraction") -RetentionDuration TwelveMonths -Priority 100

# Step 3: Configure Sentinel data connector for M365 Copilot logs
# Step 4: Create analytics rule for JailbreakDetected=true (every 15 min)
# Step 5: Schedule weekly review of top users by volume and sensitivity
cop-005.validationNotes:
# Verify audit logging is enabled
Get-AdminAuditLogConfig | Select UnifiedAuditLogIngestionEnabled
# Expected: True

# Search for recent Copilot interactions
Search-UnifiedAuditLog -StartDate (Get-Date).AddDays(-1) -EndDate (Get-Date) `
  -Operations "CopilotInteraction" -ResultSize 10 |
  Select CreationDate, UserIds

# Verify retention policy exists
Get-UnifiedAuditLogRetentionPolicy | Where-Object { $_.Name -like "*Copilot*" } |
  Select Name, RetentionDuration, Priority
cop-005.tuningNotes: Copilot interaction logs can be high volume in large organizations. Focus Sentinel analytics rules on high-risk patterns — jailbreak attempts, access to "Highly Confidential" labeled resources, and unusual query volumes from single users. Set up workbook dashboards for weekly security review rather than alerting on every interaction.

cop-006.attackPerspective: SharePoint oversharing is the root cause of most Copilot data exposure incidents. In typical enterprises, 40-60% of SharePoint sites have "Everyone Except External Users" sharing links created years ago. Copilot surfaces this content immediately — a user asking "what salary does the VP of Engineering make?" will get an answer if any shared file contains that data. SAM's Restricted Access Control and Restricted Content Discovery are the primary technical controls to prevent this amplification.
cop-006.implementationNotes:
# Step 1: Disable EEEU from People Picker
Connect-SPOService -Url https://<tenant>-admin.sharepoint.com
Set-SPOTenant -ShowEveryoneExceptExternalUsersClaim $false

# Step 2: Set default sharing to "Specific people"
Set-SPOTenant -DefaultSharingLinkType Direct

# Step 3: Run Data Access Governance reports for top overshared sites
# Step 4: Apply RAC to sites classified "Confidential" or higher
# Step 5: Apply RCD to sites needing Copilot exclusion
# Step 6: Enable Site Access Reviews for sites with >100 unique permissions
# Step 7: Create inactive site lifecycle policy (180 days archive)
cop-006.validationNotes:
# Verify EEEU is hidden from People Picker
Get-SPOTenant | Select ShowEveryoneExceptExternalUsersClaim
# Expected: False

# Verify default sharing link type
Get-SPOTenant | Select DefaultSharingLinkType
# Expected: Direct (Specific people)

# Check RAC status on sensitive sites
Get-SPOSite -Identity "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/Finance" |
  Select Url, RestrictedAccessControl, RestrictedContentDiscovery
cop-006.tuningNotes: RAC is aggressive — it overrides ALL existing permissions. Users with direct file links will lose access unless they're in the RAC security group. Before enabling RAC, notify site owners and give a 2-week grace period. RCD is less disruptive — it only affects search/Copilot discovery while preserving direct access. Use RCD as a first step, then RAC for truly sensitive sites.
cop-006.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Monitor sites with oversharing indicators
SharePointAuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where Operation == "SharingSet" or Operation == "SharingInvitationCreated"
| extend SharedWith = tostring(TargetUserOrGroupName)
| where SharedWith has "Everyone" or SharedWith has "Company"
| summarize OvershareCount=count() by SiteUrl, SharedWith
| sort by OvershareCount desc

cop-007.attackPerspective: RAC and RCD provide granular site-level control that goes beyond traditional SharePoint permissions. Without RCD, a sensitive site's content appears in Copilot results even if the user found it indirectly through related documents. Without RAC, users who were shared individual files from a sensitive site can still have Copilot summarize the broader context. Both controls are essential for M&A war rooms, legal hold sites, and executive communication sites where even metadata exposure is unacceptable.
cop-007.implementationNotes:
# Step 1: Identify sites requiring RAC/RCD (M&A, Legal, HR, Board)
# Step 2: Create dedicated security group per site in Entra ID
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Group.ReadWrite.All"
New-MgGroup -DisplayName "RAC-LegalHold" -MailEnabled:$false -SecurityEnabled:$true

# Step 3: Apply RAC
Set-SPOSite -Identity "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/LegalHold" `
  -RestrictedAccessControl $true -RestrictedAccessControlGroups "<GroupId>"

# Step 4: Apply RCD for Copilot isolation
Set-SPOSite -Identity "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/LegalHold" `
  -RestrictedContentDiscovery $true

# Step 5: Block downloads on M&A sites
# Step 6: Document all RAC/RCD sites in security register, review quarterly
# Step 7: Notify site owners 2 weeks before enforcement
cop-007.validationNotes:
# Verify RAC and RCD on all sensitive sites
$sensitiveSites = @(
  "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/LegalHold",
  "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/MandA",
  "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/ExecutiveComms"
)
foreach ($site in $sensitiveSites) {
  Get-SPOSite -Identity $site |
    Select Url, RestrictedAccessControl, RestrictedContentDiscovery, BlockDownloadPolicy
}

# Test: With a non-RAC user, try to ask Copilot about content in a RAC site
# Copilot should NOT surface any results from that site
cop-007.tuningNotes: RAC overrides granular file-level permissions. If a user was directly shared a file from a RAC-protected site but is NOT in the RAC group, they lose access. Communicate changes to site owners 2 weeks before enforcement. For sites needing read-only broad access but no Copilot discovery, use RCD alone without RAC.
cop-007.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Monitor access attempts to RAC-protected sites
SharePointAuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where SiteUrl in~ ("<sensitive-site-urls>")
| where Operation == "FileAccessed" or Operation == "PageViewed"
| summarize AccessCount=count() by UserId, SiteUrl, Operation
| sort by AccessCount desc

cop-008.attackPerspective: When Copilot web search is enabled, user prompts effectively become search queries sent externally. While Microsoft states prompts aren't shared with Bing, the semantic index still correlates internal data with web results — creating data correlation risks. For organizations handling classified or regulated data, web search allows Copilot to combine internal context with external information, potentially revealing internal project names, vendor relationships, or strategic initiatives through the nature of the queries. Disabling web search limits Copilot to organizational data only.
cop-008.implementationNotes:
# Step 1: Disable web search (M365 admin > Copilot > Settings > Web search)
# Step 2: Verify via Graph API
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "OrgSettings-Microsoft365Copilot.ReadWrite.All"
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/admin/microsoft365/copilot/settings"

# Step 3: Disable third-party plugins (Settings > Plugins)
# Step 4: Monitor web search usage over 30 days
# Step 5: Review quarterly as Microsoft adds new toggles
cop-008.validationNotes:
# Verify web search setting via Graph API
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "OrgSettings-Microsoft365Copilot.ReadWrite.All"
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/admin/microsoft365/copilot/settings"
# Check: isWebSearchEnabled should be false for classified environments

# Monitor web search usage in audit logs
Connect-IPPSSession
Search-UnifiedAuditLog -StartDate (Get-Date).AddDays(-7) -EndDate (Get-Date) `
  -Operations "CopilotInteraction" -ResultSize 100 |
  Where-Object { $_.AuditData -like "*BingWebSearch*" } |
  Measure-Object
# Expected: Count = 0 if web search is disabled
cop-008.tuningNotes: Disabling web search reduces Copilot usefulness for research tasks. Some teams (marketing, competitive intelligence) may need web search. Consider a phased approach — disable tenant-wide, then selectively enable for specific groups via admin controls as Microsoft releases group-scoped web search settings.
cop-008.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Monitor Copilot web search usage patterns
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where OperationName == "CopilotInteraction"
| extend Detail = parse_json(AdditionalDetails)
| where tostring(Detail) has "BingWebSearch"
| summarize WebSearchCount=count() by UserPrincipalName, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| sort by WebSearchCount desc
